data.title: Alibaba Q4 Preview: AI, cloud, U.S.-China trade deal fuel optimism
data.description:
Analysts expect Alibaba’s (NYSE:BABA) revenue growth to accelerate during its fourth-quarter earnings report, scheduled for Wednesday, May 14, before the market opens. Their optimism is driven not only by the company’s core e-commerce business but also by its AI and cloud computing segments.

Wall Street is eyeing a 27% year-on-year jump in EPS to $1.78 while revenue is expected to rise by 8.43% compared to last year to $33.32B.

BABA has surged more than 57% year-to-date, fueled by investor enthusiasm surrounding AI advancements and the recently finalized U.S.-China trade deal.

Seeking Alpha analyst Dair Sansyzbayev also believes that the trade deal and a bullish outlook on Alibaba's cloud and AI business could serve as significant positive catalysts for the Chinese e-commerce giant’s Q4 results.

The analyst noted that the company’s cloud and AI segment has quickly emerged as the primary driver of revenue growth and he expects to hear more optimistic commentary about its performance during the upcoming earnings call.

Another analyst A.J. Button, echoed similar sentiments saying that “Alibaba Cloud is currently #1 in Chinese cloud services. Its main virtue compared to the U.S. cloud giants is cheaper pricing. Within China, it is also price-competitive.

“In addition, Alibaba is generally ahead of the competition in Chinese AI models. Broadly speaking, Alibaba appears to be ahead of everyone in China except maybe DeepSeek. Since DeepSeek does not compete with Alibaba Cloud, and even provides a use case for BABA's cloud (running DeepSeek on Alibaba servers), Alibaba's position in Chinese AI appears very strong,” he added.

Commenting on the U.S.-China trade deal, Sansyzbayev described it as a pivotal development for both countries, citing the substantial volume of bilateral trade.

“Lower tariffs will not disrupt the trade between both global behemoths, which is an apparent positive factor for an e-commerce giant like BABA,” he said.

Over the last 2 years, BABA has beaten EPS estimates 88% of the time and surpassed revenue estimates 75% of the time. Over the last 3 months, EPS estimates have seen 10 upward revisions and 3 downward moves, whereas revenue estimates have seen 9 upward revisions and 7 downward moves.

data.title: Hyliion reiterates $10M-$15M 2025 revenue guidance as KARNO commercialization remains on track
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hyliion Holdings Corp. (HYLN) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Thomas Healy opened by highlighting the public unveiling of the KARNO Power Module at the ACT Expo and noted strong industry and customer response, stating "the response was extremely positive, and we had the opportunity to meet with many current and prospective customers." Healy emphasized that the company now refers to the generator as the KARNO Power Module and described its unique value as a "fully integrated, enclosed, fuel-agnostic power generation solution."
  • Healy reported progress on two previously discussed development challenges: production of the linear electric motor (LEM) and depowdering of complex printed parts. He noted, "we are able to revert to a more tightly packed mesh, which we believe will address the power deficit," and described successful early lab results in powder removal.
  • Healy added that Hyliion signed a non-binding LOI with Mesa Natural Gas Solution for up to 12 KARNO units and now has "well over 100 units under non-binding LOIs across a range of markets, including data centers, EV charging, waste gas utilization, industrial deployments, and military programs."
  • Healy described the Navy partnership and stated, "the system has experienced no unplanned downtime or availability issues since we began the regular operation of the unit back in March."
  • Regarding manufacturing, Healy said, "we now have over 20 additive printers installed and operational" and noted the receipt of GE's latest M-Line machine, indicating ongoing ramp in production capacity.
  • CFO Jon Panzer stated, "we recorded revenue of $500,000 for research and development services related to our contracts with the Office of Naval Research. Cost of sales was also $500,000, resulting in the operating income of approximately break-even."
  • Panzer explained, "operating expenses for the first quarter were $19.7 million, compared to $19 million in the first quarter of 2024. Research and development costs were $12.2 million, compared to $8 million in 2024."

Outlook

  • Healy reiterated full-year 2025 guidance: "We remain on track to commercially launch the KARNO power module by year-end, and we continue to expect revenue between $10 million and $15 million for full year 2025, driven by early adopter unit deployments and R&D activities."
  • Panzer added, "we expect R&D revenue to be higher in future quarters this year as deployments resume."
  • Panzer maintained, "we previously expected that capital expenditures for 2025 will be approximately $25 million... We now expect capital expenditures could be closer to $30 million." Total cash outlays expected to be $65 million, with a year-end cash and investment balance of approximately $155 million.
  • Panzer confirmed, "our current outlook for achieving break-even gross margin on a cash basis is near the end of 2026."

Financial Results

  • Panzer reported $500,000 in first-quarter revenue, all from R&D services with the Office of Naval Research. Cost of sales matched revenue at $500,000.
  • Operating expenses in Q1 reached $19.7 million; research and development costs were $12.2 million, and SG&A expenses were $6.1 million.
  • Exit and termination costs related to the former powertrain business were $1.4 million.
  • Net loss for the quarter was $17.3 million.
  • Cash and short- and long-term investments at quarter-end were $198.8 million.
  • Capital spending was $7.3 million, primarily for additional printing machines.

Q&A

  • Greg Standley: "Can you tell us more about the Navy's unit performance?" Healy responded that the Navy's KARNO core has been running daily at the Cincinnati facility: "We've seen very consistent reliability out of it. So since we started running it a couple of months ago, we haven't had to take the KARNO core out of the power module at all. We haven't had to tear it down, do any rebuilds of it."
  • Standley: "Given where you are and the challenges that you discussed today, how will this impact your growth plans for next year?" Healy replied, "we are still on track to be able to move into commercialization late this year, which then we don't envision has an impact into the amount of units and scale up of production that we'll do next year."
  • Healy noted that manufacturing bottlenecks, specifically with LEMs, "has really been what's prevented us from shipping more units to date," but the company is supplementing contract manufacturing with in-house assembly to address supply constraints.
  • Healy highlighted a recent company visit to Saudi Arabia in partnership with a local firm, with more details expected in coming days.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral, primarily seeking clarification on technical progress, reliability, and potential delivery impacts from production issues.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, as seen in statements like, "we are confident in our ability to ramp production here in Austin" and "we remain on track to commercially launch the KARNO power module by year-end."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remains confident, with continued emphasis on reliability and mitigation of development challenges. Analyst sentiment did not shift materially between quarters.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 revenue remains at $10 million to $15 million, unchanged from the previous quarter.
  • Commercial launch of the KARNO Power Module is still targeted for late 2025, in line with prior statements.
  • Key production bottlenecks identified last quarter, specifically LEM manufacturing and depowdering, saw reported progress but continue to impact near-term deployments, slightly delaying some unit deliveries into the second half of the year.
  • Manufacturing investments have increased, with capital expenditures now expected to reach $30 million, compared to prior guidance of $25 million.
  • Net loss for the quarter increased from $14.4 million in Q4 2024 to $17.3 million in Q1 2025.
  • Management sentiment remains optimistic, focusing on reliability, supply chain solutions, and commercialization timeline. Analyst focus remains on technical progress and production scaling.

Risks and Concerns

  • Panzer identified exposure to tariffs, particularly on additive printers from GE assembled in Germany, estimating a $2 million to $3 million increase in capital spending.
  • Some additive printer components sourced overseas may also be subject to tariffs, potentially increasing costs.
  • R&D expenses are running higher than expected due to accelerated component sourcing and addressing production issues.
  • Delays in LEM production and depowdering have shifted some early adopter unit deployments into the second half of 2025.
  • Management is implementing in-house assembly and advanced cleaning methods to mitigate supply chain and technical risks.

Final Takeaway

Hyliion’s first quarter 2025 was marked by the successful public debut of the KARNO Power Module, strong reliability data from the Navy collaboration, and steady customer interest evidenced by numerous LOIs. Although supply chain and technical hurdles have shifted some early deployments later into the year, management maintains guidance for $10 million to $15 million in 2025 revenue and the planned commercial launch by year-end. The company’s strengthened manufacturing capabilities, ongoing resolution of production challenges, and robust cash position are presented as key assets supporting progress toward its commercialization and growth objectives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: New York state sues Capital One after CFPB drops case
data.description:
New York State's attorney general sued Capital One Financial (NYSE:COF) and its bank subsidiary, Capital One N.A., after the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, under the Trump administration, dropped its case against the bank.

The New York suit alleges that the bank created a two-tiered system of saving accounts that misled customers regarding its interest rates, similar to the allegations made by the CFPB when it sued Capital One (NYSE:COF) in the last week of the Biden administration.

New York Attorney General Letitia James alleged that Capital One (COF) marketed its "360 Savings" accounts as high interest accounts with "one of the nation's best savings rates."  Instead, the interest rates on its 360 Savings accounts were kept "artificially low," the suit alleged. A different account, "360 Performance Savings," provided a much higher interest rate.

"Capital One intentionally misled its 360 Savings customers about the existence of its 360 Performance Savings product to avoid paying them millions of dollars in interest," the New York attorney general's office said in its press release.

The lawsuit contends that, "While Capital One promoted its 360 Performance Savings accounts to existing customers, it did not notify its 360 Savings customers of the chance to earn more interest." It also alleged that Capital One (COF) employees were told not to tell 360 Savings customers about the new product unless they explicitly asked.

In January, when the CFPB filed the lawsuit, a company spokesperson said it strongly disagreed with the CFPB allegations, which were nearly identical with the New York state claims. The person also said the 360 Performance account was widely marketed, including on national television.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: ADC Therapeutics signals data catalysts ahead with ZYNLONTA combinations, aims for regulatory milestones through 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ADC Therapeutics SA (ADCT) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ameet Mallik reported that "the first quarter of 2025 represented a solid period of continued performance for our company," highlighting total first quarter revenues of $23 million, with net product revenues of $17.4 million. He noted these sales were in line with the same quarter last year and compared favorably to the $16.4 million in the previous quarter.
  • Mallik emphasized the acceptance of data from LOTIS-7 for presentation at both the European Hematology Association Congress and the International Conference on Malignant Lymphoma in June, underscoring that "ZYNLONTA plus glofitamab demonstrated an overall response rate of 95.5% and a complete response rate of 90.9% in the 22 efficacy-evaluable patients" as of January 2025. He noted plans to share further data updates in the second half of 2025 and the potential for enrollment expansion to 100 patients to support regulatory discussions.
  • Mallik confirmed the discontinuation of the ADCT602 trial in relapsed or refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia, while highlighting preclinical progress on exatecan-based ADCs targeting Claudin-6, PSMA, and ASCT2.
  • CFO Pepe Carmona stated, "ZYNLONTA net product revenues in the first quarter of 2025 were $17.4 million as compared to $17.8 million in the same quarter of 2024." He reported total operating expenses of $49.1 million on a non-GAAP basis for the quarter and a net loss of $38.6 million, or $0.36 per share, compared to a net loss of $46.6 million, or $0.56 per share, in Q1 2024. Carmona added, "As of March 31, 2025, cash and cash equivalents were $194.7 million, compared to $250.9 million on December 31, 2024."

Outlook

  • Mallik affirmed, "We are encouraged by the results we've reported so far and are assessing options for expanding enrollment to 100 patients at the recommended dose level, which will support regulatory discussions." He stated that the company expects to share an additional update on LOTIS-7 in the second half of 2025 and anticipates reaching the pre-specified number of progression-free survival events in LOTIS-5 by the end of 2025, with top-line data to follow.
  • Carmona reiterated, "With an expected cash runway into the second half of 2026, I'm confident that ADC Therapeutics is well-positioned to deliver on our catalysts and drive value creation for all our stakeholders."

Financial Results

  • ZYNLONTA net product revenues stood at $17.4 million for Q1 2025, compared to $17.8 million in Q1 2024 and $16.4 million in Q4 2024. Total revenues for the quarter reached $23 million, which includes $5 million in licensing revenue related to ZYNLONTA's approval by Health Canada.
  • Operating expenses were $49.1 million on a non-GAAP basis, marking a 5% net decrease over the prior year, mainly from reduced SG&A expenses. The company reported a net loss of $38.6 million for the quarter, improved from a net loss of $46.6 million in the same period a year earlier.
  • As of March 31, 2025, cash and cash equivalents were $194.7 million. The reported change from year-end reflects operational net losses and various timing factors in cash receipts and payments.

Q&A

  • Unidentified Analyst (Jefferies): Asked about follow-up time and durability of response in LOTIS-7. Mohamed Zaki responded that while it is "too early to speak about durability or the degree of follow-up at this stage," the high number of complete responses is a strong biomarker for durability, with more data expected at EHA.
  • Unidentified Analyst (Jefferies): Inquired about compendia strategy timing. Mallik said, "We believe we're going to need a publication including the approximately 100 patients with about a year of follow-up," referencing recent guideline additions for similar regimens.
  • Eric Schmidt (Cantor): Sought details on patient numbers for EHA and on the efficacy/safety profile of ZYNLONTA plus glofitamab. Mallik confirmed 40 patients enrolled, with more than 22 likely presented, but specifics withheld due to embargo. He stated, "From an efficacy standpoint, we think the data is very, very encouraging right now," and noted the regimen's unique mechanism and manageable safety.
  • Unidentified Analyst (Guggenheim): Asked about LOTIS-5 timing and competitive benchmarks. Mallik reiterated that top-line data may come at year-end or early 2026, depending on PFS events. Zaki described the trial's design and success criteria, referencing early data showing 50% complete response and 80% overall response rate.
  • Sudan Loganathan (Stephens): Asked about the regulatory pathway and pipeline capital allocation. Zaki confirmed plans to meet with regulators in the second half of the year; Carmona noted that discontinuing the 602 program has low cost impact and business development efforts for the preclinical pipeline are ongoing.
  • Unidentified Analyst (RBC): Queried on upcoming data presentations and expectations for LOTIS-5 and LOTIS-7. Zaki said more patient data and longer follow-up will be presented, but further details are embargoed. For LOTIS-5, durability of complete responses exceeding two years will be shared.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts generally expressed positive sentiment, with multiple congratulations on clinical progress and requests for further data details, particularly on response durability, regulatory milestones, and competitive positioning.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, consistently highlighting strong efficacy signals, manageable safety, and multiple upcoming catalysts. Confidence was reinforced by phrases such as "we are encouraged," "we are confident," and "I'm excited about the multiple upcoming catalysts ahead."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the sentiment remained upbeat, with heightened focus on the exceptional response rates in LOTIS-7 and tangible progress towards regulatory and data milestones.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remains consistent, with continued emphasis on advancing ZYNLONTA combinations and pursuing regulatory/compendia strategies. The current quarter added detail on LOTIS-7 efficacy rates and the plan to potentially expand enrollment for regulatory support.
  • Key financial metrics were stable, with product revenues roughly flat and operating expenses declining. Net loss improved from the prior year.
  • Analysts' focus shifted slightly more towards the durability of clinical response and the specifics of regulatory strategy, while management's confidence level increased in light of the recent high response rates and positive safety data.
  • Discontinuation of the ADCT602 trial marks a shift in pipeline resource focus to ZYNLONTA and preclinical assets.

Risks and Concerns

  • The company acknowledged that it is "too early to speak about durability" for ZYNLONTA plus glofitamab responses, with long-term outcomes still pending.
  • Regulatory and compendia success depend on accumulating sufficient patient numbers and follow-up, with Mallik stating publication of approximately 100 patients with a year of follow-up is needed.
  • The discontinuation of ADCT602 demonstrates ongoing pipeline evaluation and potential for program attrition.
  • Management highlighted the importance of maintaining cash runway and managing operating expenses to support ongoing clinical development and regulatory activities.

Final Takeaway

ADC Therapeutics emphasized the strong efficacy and safety profile of ZYNLONTA combinations in ongoing clinical trials, reporting high response rates and manageable toxicity in LOTIS-7, while maintaining stable product revenues and a solid cash position. The company outlined key milestones ahead, including expanded data presentations, potential enrollment increases, and regulatory interactions, all aimed at establishing ZYNLONTA as a preferred therapy in an evolving DLBCL treatment landscape.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Scholar Rock signals Q3 US launch for apitegromab with $364.4M in cash on hand while advancing global expansion plans
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Scholar Rock Holding Corporation (SRRK) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Hallal stated, "2025 is off to a strong start at Scholar Rock. In the first quarter, we made significant progress against our top priority of bringing apitegromab to patients and families living with and suffering from SMA in the US, Europe and around the world." He highlighted the positive Phase 3 SAPPHIRE trial results, the FDA granting priority review for the apitegromab BLA, and the anticipated US commercial launch in Q3. Hallal emphasized, "Our ambition at Scholar Rock is that, any patient with SMA that can benefit from apitegromab should have access to apitegromab."
  • Hallal described ongoing preparations for global market entry: "We are also beginning to set the table to serve patients with SMA in Europe, Asia Pacific and Latin America."
  • He also noted exploratory initiatives: "We are continuing to evaluate expanding the study of apitegromab into other rare, severe and debilitating neuromuscular disorders."
  • President of R&D Akshay Vaishnaw discussed the SAPPHIRE study, stating it "showed that apitegromab has the potential to reverse the progression of SMA from a loss of motor function to a gain of motor function," with a "threefold higher chance of a three point or greater increase in Hammersmith versus those on placebo."
  • COO Keith Woods said, "With apitegromab, we have the opportunity to usher in a new era for the treatment of patients with SMA," and outlined commercial launch readiness, noting the "hiring and onboarding our customer facing team of roughly 50 sales, reimbursement and patient support personnel is well underway. We expect to be fully staffed by mid-2025 well ahead of our potential launch in late September."
  • CFO Vikas Sinha reported, "We ended the quarter with $364.4 million. During the quarter, we continued to increase our investments in commercial readiness and inventory build out." Sinha added, "We have an additional $100 million under our debt facility that we can draw down this year to support the upcoming launch, bringing our anticipated runway into 2027."

Outlook

  • Hallal indicated the US commercial launch for apitegromab is on track for Q3, with the BLA granted priority review by the FDA and a PDUFA date of September 22, 2025. He also stated, "We are focused on delivering for patients... as we advance our mission to deliver apetaglimab to patients with SMA."
  • Vaishnaw outlined, "We will, one, drive the US approval of apitegromab in Q3 2025 and advance the EU approval in 2026. Two, initiate a study of apitegromab for infants and toddlers with SMA under the age of two starting in Q3. Three, file an IND for SRK-439 in the third quarter."
  • The company remains on track to share top line EMBRAZE study results in June 2025.

Financial Results

  • Sinha reported, "We ended the quarter with $364.4 million. During the quarter, we continued to increase our investments in commercial readiness and inventory build out." He also noted access to an additional $100 million through a debt facility and an anticipated runway into 2027.
  • The company is prioritizing investments in launch, clinical programs, and pipeline development while focusing on capital discipline.

Q&A

  • Allison Bratzel, Piper Sandler: Asked about US payer discussions, combination therapy coverage, and regulatory interactions. Hallal responded that pricing will "be reflective of the rarity of the disease, the severity of the disease and the value that our therapy would provide to patients." Woods stated, "our early discussions with payers have proven to be positive," and Vaishnaw added, "the pace and the collaborative conversations we've had with the FDA are all going as per routine."
  • Michael Yee, Jefferies: Inquired about the EMPRAZE obesity data and endpoints. Vaishnaw explained, "The core of it, we'll be providing the 24 week data, that's the main study... the primary focus will be the 24 week data, change in lean mass, the safety and helping us understand the path forward."
  • Tessa Romero, JPMorgan: Asked about regulatory progress and labeling discussions. Vaishnaw replied, "conversations have been extremely constructive and right on track... our ability today to guide on the call that we're heading to that September 22 PDUFA date is based on a series of very constructive conversations."
  • Gary Nachman, Raymond James: Asked about launch preparedness and supply. Woods stated, "We are fully staffed when it comes to our marketing team and... our market access team. We will have the entire commercial team fully staffed by mid-2025."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was cautiously positive, focusing on payer coverage, regulatory progress, launch readiness, and competitive positioning. Questions probed for detail on reimbursement, supply, and commercial strategy.
  • Management maintained a confident and urgent tone throughout, repeatedly referencing "urgency," "confidence," and "focus" on launch execution and regulatory milestones. During Q&A, the tone remained constructive and collaborative, with frequent reassurances regarding FDA interactions and market access.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more assertive about launch timelines and global expansion, while analysts continued to focus on tangible commercial and regulatory execution milestones.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance became more specific, with a firm Q3 US launch timeline for apitegromab and a September 22, 2025 PDUFA date, versus more general expectations in Q4 2024.
  • Strategic focus shifted toward execution of the US launch and detailed global expansion plans, with increased discussion of commercial team buildout and market access preparations.
  • Analysts' focus remained on launch readiness and regulatory clarity, but questions in Q1 2025 centered more on payer dynamics and combination therapy coverage than in the previous quarter.
  • Management's confidence appeared heightened, with concrete milestones and capital resources highlighted, compared to the forward-looking but less detailed remarks in Q4 2024.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the evolving FDA environment, but Vaishnaw reassured, "the collaborative conversations we've had with the FDA are all going as per routine."
  • Hallal addressed potential pricing pressure, stating, "given the rarity of disease, we would expect that the budget impact to any one single payer in the US or even globally would be very limited for the high value proposition."
  • Woods noted reimbursement and formulary processes at academic centers may affect initial launch kinetics but expressed confidence in steady uptake over time.
  • Uncertainty remains on the impact of the President's executive order on pricing, but Hallal commented, "it really does not change in any way our plans to commercialize apitegromab."

Final Takeaway

Scholar Rock management underscored 2025 as a transformative year, centered around the Q3 US launch of apitegromab for SMA, supported by a strong cash position and priority FDA review. The company is actively preparing for global market entry, expanding its clinical pipeline, and maintaining a disciplined approach to capital allocation, with the stated goal of making apitegromab accessible to every eligible SMA patient worldwide.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Moleculin signals first 45-patient unblinding for Phase 3 AML trial by end of 2025 as key regulatory milestones achieved
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Moleculin Biotech (MBRX) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Walter Klemp highlighted the official start of the Phase 3 MIRACLE trial for Annamycin, with the first patient treated and a total of 38 sites now selected worldwide. He noted, "We just announced this week that we also received complete sign off from the European Medicines Agency for all nine countries that we wanted to open in the EU." Klemp also emphasized a milestone with the World Health Organization's recognition of the new generic drug name "naxtarubicin" for Annamycin and additional patent protection extending into at least 2040.
  • Klemp stated that an announcement is forthcoming on the presentation date for final data from the MB-107 clinical trial in advanced soft tissue sarcoma, indicating high expectations for the impact of the results.
  • Senior Chief Medical Officer Paul Waymack described the durability observed in the MB-106 Phase 2 trial, with one patient achieving almost two years of complete remission and three ongoing complete remissions. He outlined the MIRACLE trial's design, emphasizing, "the most important milestone for us coming up will be the unblinding of the first 45 patients."
  • CFO Jonathan Foster reported, "We ended the quarter with about $8 million with cash on hand. This should run our operations into the third quarter of this year. To get us well into the first quarter of 2026, we will need to raise approximately $15 million."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated the expectation to treat the first 45 MIRACLE trial patients before the end of 2025, a key milestone for potential new drug approval. Klemp explained, "just averaging one patient per site for the remainder of the year gets us there. And given the enthusiasm that we've seen from these sites, we think we're going to outpace that rate."
  • The company expects to release a final data readout on MB-107 in the coming weeks and indicated ongoing progress on WP1066 clinical development, including a new IV formulation in collaboration with Emory University.
  • There was no explicit change in guidance language versus the previous quarter, with the timeline for the interim data readout and overall strategic objectives reaffirmed.

Financial Results

  • Foster stated Moleculin ended the quarter with approximately $8 million in cash, noting, "this should run our operations into the third quarter of this year." He indicated an additional $15 million will be required to fund the company into the first quarter of 2026 and through key clinical milestones.
  • The market cap was reported at over $14 million with 14.1 million shares outstanding, and a three-month trading volume of almost 6 million shares per day.
  • Foster highlighted recent achievements in site contracting and regulatory approvals as major contributors to operational progress.

Q&A

  • Jonathan Aschoff, ROTH Capital Partners: "The statement about the results will be submitted as a substantial modification to the EMA, does that have any negative implications for the timeline of EU approval versus U.S.?" Klemp responded, "we believe we will get to the requisite number of EU patients to satisfy EU approval requirements. So the answer is, in theory, it shouldn't change the timeline for approval for EMA, but we have to acknowledge that depending on the time it takes to get those GLP studies done, that it could, but that's not our expectation."
  • Aschoff: "Is that $3.5 million sort of a fair run rate for R&D for the rest of the year's quarters, or is that, I mean, that should go up, shouldn't it?" Foster replied, "Yes, it'll go up, especially as we head into 2026 when we bring on the GLP and some manufacturing expenses."
  • Jason McCarthy, Maxim Group: "Do you need any durability data for potential approval, or is one month enough?" Waymack answered, "FDA has said that's their primary endpoint... There should be a strong trend. This is because that's what the FDA asked for."
  • McCarthy: "Could results be impacted by underlying factors like age and mutational genomic alterations?" Klemp responded, "for sure, we will stratify by age and by genetic mutations, but our primary endpoint isn't dependent upon it."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on regulatory timelines, R&D spending, and durability endpoints, showing a neutral to cautiously optimistic tone while probing potential hurdles and clinical expectations.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently emphasizing milestones and regulatory progress. Klemp stated, "The key milestones are coming quickly this year, so stay tuned for a wild ride at Moleculin."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remained optimistic, but with heightened emphasis on execution and imminent clinical milestones.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter highlighted new regulatory approvals, including EMA signoff for nine EU countries, a step up from the prior quarter's focus on site selection and early approvals.
  • Progression from patient screening to active enrollment and initial treatments in MIRACLE marked clear advancement.
  • Cash on hand decreased from approximately $13 million (including February capital raise) in Q4 2024 to $8 million in Q1 2025, with R&D spending expected to increase.
  • Management reiterated its confidence in reaching key patient enrollment targets and regulatory milestones, with analysts maintaining a steady focus on clinical trial logistics and financial runway.
  • Strategic focus remained consistent, but with added emphasis on global harmonization of protocols and advancing secondary pipeline assets.

Risks and Concerns

  • Klemp acknowledged potential delays in EU trial progression due to required GLP preclinical studies, though he stated, "that's not our expectation."
  • Foster identified a need to raise $15 million to fund operations and reach the next major data readout.
  • Analysts probed the sufficiency of clinical endpoints and the impact of external variables such as age and genetic mutations, with management expressing confidence in current trial design and data trends.

Final Takeaway

Moleculin Biotech’s leadership underscored rapid progress on its pivotal Phase 3 MIRACLE trial, achieving full regulatory signoff in the EU and advancing global patient enrollment. The company expects to reach the critical milestone of unblinding data after treating 45 patients before year-end 2025, a step that could significantly influence its regulatory trajectory and market outlook. With a diversified pipeline and a focused approach to operational milestones, management projects strong momentum while acknowledging the need for additional funding and continued execution on trial logistics and regulatory requirements.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nasdaq up nearly 1%, S&P slightly higher as Wall Street tries to extend rally
data.description:
Wall Street on Wednesday was largely higher and was looking to extend a two-day rally that turned the benchmark S&P 500 index positive for the year.

The S&P (SP500) was last +0.19% at 5,897.77 points in midday trade. In the previous session, the gauge ended in positive territory for 2025 for the first time since February 28. 

The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.70% at 19,143.05 points, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.06% at 42,167.05 points.

A surprise trade deal between the U.S. and China on Monday, followed by a benign consumer inflation report on Tuesday, proved as the final catalysts to push Wall Street into the green for the year.

"The stock market's rally has legs, as the trade negotiation with China was seemingly the toughest one on the docket, and the idea that there has been this much progress on the negotiations over such a short period of time, suggests that a resolution may be on the horizon," Rick Gardner, chief investment officer at RGA Investments, said.

Since U.S. President Donald Trump's "Liberation Day" announcement, the S&P has experienced one of its swiftest descents into bear market territory to completely erasing all tariff-related losses to finally eking out YTD gains - a journey of dizzying extremes. From its April 52-week session low, the gauge has rebounded a whopping +21.75%. 

"What is remarkable about the market's rebound since the April lows is the leadership of the technology sector, which was not leading the market in the early months of 2025 before the tariff situation escalated. We anticipate tech may continue to lead this market, as the sector stands to benefit from easing trade tensions and as investors once again resume their excitement over the promise of artificial intelligence," Gardner added.

The focus on Wednesday was on Trump's ongoing visit to the Middle East. The President visited Qatar, a day after securing $600B in investment commitments from Saudi Arabia. Trump said Qatar had agreed to buy 160 of U.S. planemaker Boeing's (BA) jets worth more than $200B. 

Looking at active movers, Super Micro Computer (SMCI) surged +18% after the AI server maker entered into a multi-year $20B partnership with Saudi Arabia-based data center DataVolt.  

Turning to the fixed-income markets, U.S. Treasury yields were higher. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was up 5 basis points to 4.52%, while the shorter-end more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was up 3 basis points to 4.05%. 

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

data.title: Similarweb outlines 15% revenue growth target for 2025 while expanding AI product suite and ramping sales investments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Similarweb Ltd. (SMWB) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Or Offer highlighted a 14% revenue increase to $67 million and a 19% year-over-year customer base growth, reaching over 5,700 ARR customers at quarter end. Offer noted, "We doubled the number of inside sale reps selling in this quarter, as compared to Q1 last year and our progress on releasing new features like GenAI, traffic intelligence and free AI agents, our staff to be proud of."
  • Offer reported strong early adoption for new products, specifically App Intelligence, stating, "We launched App Intelligence, expanding our app data and incorporating the acquisition of 42matters last year. I'm super happy that Similarweb can now provide digital data on more than 4 million iOS and Android mobile apps in 58 countries." He revealed that 484 customers had signed up for App Intelligence.
  • Offer described the launch of the AI Chatbot traffic intelligence and a suite of AI agents, emphasizing customer excitement and pipeline growth for these offerings.
  • CFO Jason Schwartz stated, "We generated $67.1 million of revenue in Q1, a 14% increase relative to Q1 '24. Revenue growth was driven by the 19% growth in customers, mainly in the below $100,000 ARR cohort, as well as expansions and upsells from our over $100,000 ARR customers."
  • Schwartz explained that 52% of ARR is now under multi-year contracts and noted, "Our remaining performance obligations or RPO totaled $253 million at the end of Q1, up 18% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • Schwartz confirmed, "For the full-year 2025, we are maintaining our previous guidance and expect total revenue in the range of $285 million to $288 million, representing 15% year-over-year growth at the midpoint of the range and expect our non-GAAP operating profit to be between $1 million and $4 million."
  • Q2 2025 guidance is for total revenue between $68.6 million and $69 million, with a non-GAAP operating loss expected in the range of $0.5 million to $1 million.
  • Management reiterated a focus on profitable growth and readiness to adjust investment if planned returns do not materialize.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q1 2025 was $67.1 million, up 14% from Q1 2024.
  • Normalized free cash flow was $5 million, marking the sixth consecutive quarter of positive free cash flow and a 7% free cash flow margin.
  • Non-GAAP operating loss was 2% in Q1, attributed to increased investments in sales and R&D.
  • NRR for over $100,000 customers increased by 400 basis points year-over-year to 111% and for the overall base increased by 300 basis points to 101%.
  • 52% of ARR is under multi-year contracts, up from 42% a year ago.

Q&A

  • Arjun Bhatia, William Blair: Asked about the ramp to productivity for new sales hires. Offer responded, "We're very happy from the quality. We've earned a lot over the past few years about how we can bring the right persona to sell our specific offering... right now looking good."
  • Bhatia: Inquired about AI chatbot traffic product usage. Offer said, "It's very unique. So it's very nice to see that it's feel like, in the early days when we launched similar web and felt like magic... the reaction is super nice."
  • Damon Cavan (for Barclays): Asked about revenue visibility for H2. Offer detailed different ramp times depending on sales roles, stating, "In the inside sales organization, you can see much faster the ramp and you can recognize the yield. The other was taking more time by nature."
  • Cavan: Asked about the impact of the Search Monitor acquisition. Schwartz replied, "Nothing material on that. There was a small business where we're excited to have him on board, not material contribution for the quarter or the year."
  • Surinder Thind, Jefferies: Questioned trends among larger and SMB clients. Offer noted that the decline in NRR among $100,000 customers was due to a tough comp from prior upsells and highlighted strong demand and increased multi-year engagement in the SMB segment.
  • Jason Helfstein, Oppenheimer: Asked about selling AI products and strategy under new CRO. Offer described broad excitement for AI offerings across customer personas and noted the CRO had completed her first year, with expectations for execution on recent changes.
  • Tyler Radke, Citi: Inquired about bookings and RPO trends. Schwartz explained seasonal patterns and large deal renewals, saying, "Current RPO was up 9% year-over-year, but we do think of that as somewhat seasonal."
  • Adam Hotchkiss, Goldman Sachs: Asked about key execution priorities. Offer identified upsell motion in enterprise accounts as most important and cited success in data as a service and ChatAI data sales.
  • Austin Cole (for Patrick Walravens, Citizens): Asked about engagement with the AI Chatbot product. Offer said, "Right now we opened traffic GenAI as a beta to our paying yearly customer... this model really helped convert a lot of leads or credit card customer to yearly."
  • Scott Berg, Needham: Queried expansion activity. Schwartz shared a customer case where a U.S. retailer quadrupled spend in two years, demonstrating expansion potential.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, with questions primarily focused on execution, product adoption, and sales ramp timelines. Concerns were expressed about visibility into H2 growth, deal flows, and the impact of new hires, but responses were met with detailed explanations and confidence from management.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, using phrases like "we are just getting started" and referencing strong customer reactions and pipeline momentum. Management was prepared to address concerns about sales productivity and investment returns directly and provided specific examples to support their positions.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's sentiment remained positive but demonstrated a higher focus on operational execution and ROI from recent investments. Analysts’ tone showed more focus on near-term execution risks than in the previous call.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 revenue and operating profit remained unchanged from Q4, signaling steady expectations despite increased investment.
  • Strategic focus shifted from announcing major new partnerships (e.g., Bloomberg, S&P Global in Q4) to emphasizing product launches and operational execution in Q1.
  • Management highlighted tangible progress in ramping sales capacity and launching AI-driven products, while last quarter focused more on setting the stage for these investments.
  • Q1 emphasized validation of recent investments through customer adoption metrics and early sales productivity, while Q4 highlighted the initiation of these investments and partnerships.
  • Analysts' questions in Q1 focused more on measuring the ROI and productivity of hires and the uptake of new AI products, compared to Q4's focus on the reasoning behind the step-up in spend and long-term growth targets.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the risk that sales and R&D investments may not deliver planned returns, with Schwartz stating preparedness "to rapidly respond and improve profitability as we have in the past."
  • Currency volatility and macroeconomic uncertainty remain present, but no material impact has been seen to date.
  • Analysts sought clarity on deal visibility, sales rep productivity, and the conversion of new products, indicating concerns about execution risk and near-term growth realization.
  • Management reiterated readiness to reduce unproductive capacity if investments underperform, referencing prior success in adjusting cost structures.

Final Takeaway

Similarweb management emphasized steady progress in revenue growth, early momentum from new AI and data products, and disciplined investment in expanding the sales force. The company reaffirmed its 2025 guidance and highlighted strong customer interest in AI-driven solutions, while also addressing operational execution risks and outlining clear metrics and contingency plans to ensure profitable growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: DTI outlines $145M–$165M 2025 revenue target amid proactive cost reductions and $10M share buyback authorization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Drilling Tools International Corporation (DTI) (DTI) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Wayne Prejean, Chief Executive Officer, reported sequential and year-over-year revenue growth and steady adjusted EBITDA, despite industry headwinds. Prejean highlighted that "revenue grew 16% over last year’s first quarter and was up nearly 8% over 2024 fourth quarter results. Adjusted EBITDA grew nearly 18% year-over-year and was flat sequentially." He stated that DTI has not yet seen tangible disruptions in North America but noted "increased volatility and uncertainty in the marketplace due to the impact of tariffs, a potential recession... and OPEC+’s decision to increase production." In response, DTI has initiated a two-phase strategy: negotiating with suppliers/customers for stability and implementing a multi-level internal cost reduction program, with Phase 1 expected to result in $6 million in annual cost reductions starting Q2.
  • Prejean announced, "our Board of Directors has unanimously approved a share buyback authorization... up to $10 million of buybacks. We believe our undervalued stock price presents one of the most compelling return on investment opportunities."
  • David Johnson, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "Despite continued rig count softness and market choppiness in the first quarter of 2025, revenue increased over last year’s first quarter by 16% in the face of a 6% global rig count decline... We generated total consolidated revenue of $42.9 million, comprised of tool rental revenue of approximately $34.5 million and product sales revenue of $8.3 million. We reported total operating expenses of $39.6 million and operating income was $3.3 million." Johnson also disclosed, "The first quarter adjusted EBITDA was $10.8 million and adjusted free cash flow was $5.7 million. At the end of the first quarter, we had approximately $2.8 million in cash and cash equivalents and net debt of $52.1 million."
  • Johnson discussed a non-cash goodwill impairment of approximately $1.9 million, attributed to the Vernal, Utah bit repair operations and Deep Casing Tools, noting it "does not affect our day-to-day operations or our ability to execute on our strategic priorities."

Outlook

  • Johnson presented revised 2025 guidance reflecting a "further decline in the North American land rig counts." Full year 2025 revenue is now expected to be in the range of $145 million to $165 million, adjusted EBITDA in the range of $32 million to $42 million, gross capital expenditures between $18 million and $23 million, and adjusted free cash flow between $14 million to $19 million. He stated, "our previous assumption of a flat to slightly up market has shifted to a down market for the remainder of 2025."

Financial Results

  • DTI reported total consolidated revenue of $42.9 million, with tool rental revenue at $34.5 million and product sales at $8.3 million for Q1 2025. Total operating expenses were $39.6 million, operating income was $3.3 million, adjusted EBITDA was $10.8 million, and adjusted free cash flow was $5.7 million. Maintenance CapEx was about 10% of total revenue. Johnson noted, "Although up slightly in Q1, this portion of our capital investment has trended lower in the past several quarters due to the decline in rig count."
  • The company recorded a non-cash goodwill impairment of approximately $1.9 million. The Eastern Hemisphere accounted for 11% of Q1 revenue, with expectations for this share to increase as the year progresses.

Q&A

  • Steve Ferazani, Sidoti & Company, questioned how DTI can "maintain pretty good free cash flow in this environment." Johnson responded, "focusing on the cost reductions to preserve as much of the EBITDA margins as we can obviously helps... making sure we coincide any purchases or defer same... to make sure we preserve that ability to generate the free cash flow."
  • Ferazani asked about Eastern Hemisphere growth, particularly regarding Deep Casing Tools in Saudi Arabia and the Middle East. Prejean explained, "the Saudi rig reduction in their offshore market... was impactful... But we’ve managed to pivot and see some consumption in their other areas," and highlighted traction in new technologies and products, such as the MechLOK Swivel and Rubblizer.
  • Ferazani inquired about North American market cadence, to which Prejean noted softness in the U.S. but strength in Canada, saying, "we have a strong business in Canada and very, very solid and sustainable operation there."
  • Ferazani sought clarity on capital allocation and interest expense. Johnson stated, "depending on the capital spend and where we exercise that free cash flow deployment, we have an opportunity to lower our debt if we pull back on the CapEx and adjust according to the activity."
  • Josh Jayne, Daniel Energy Partners, asked about North American regional risks and CapEx priorities. Prejean said, "the resiliency of each area is going to be challenged here in the next few months if oil prices keep dropping," while emphasizing flexibility and a focus on new technology and growth-oriented capital allocation.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts signaled a slightly negative to cautious tone, focusing on free cash flow sustainability, regional market volatility, and capital allocation amid market softness.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive stance, emphasizing cost control and flexibility. Prejean stated, "we are proactively negotiating with our suppliers and our customers to ensure stability and profitability."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone shifted from optimism about growth and acquisitions to more caution and a focus on cost controls and risk mitigation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 was revised downward: revenue now $145 million to $165 million (previously $163 million to $183 million), adjusted EBITDA $32 million to $42 million (previously $40 million to $50 million), and adjusted free cash flow $14 million to $19 million (previously $17 million to $21 million).
  • Strategic focus has shifted from aggressive expansion and M&A integration to cost reduction and operational flexibility.
  • Analyst questions in the current quarter concentrated more on risk management, free cash flow, and regional exposure, compared to M&A and international expansion in the previous quarter.
  • Management's confidence shifted from growth-oriented language to a more defensive, cost-focused approach, reflecting broader industry uncertainty.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited increased volatility from tariffs, potential recession, and OPEC+ production decisions as key challenges.
  • Anticipated U.S. rig count declines and pricing pressures are expected to impact margins and activity.
  • A non-cash goodwill impairment of $1.9 million was recorded but described as not impacting liquidity or operational execution.
  • Management outlined proactive mitigation strategies, including supplier/customer negotiations, cost reductions, and the flexibility to curtail growth CapEx if market conditions deteriorate.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about regional exposure, especially in North America, and the ability to sustain free cash flow amid declining activity.

Final Takeaway

DTI’s management responded to a challenging market environment by lowering 2025 guidance, launching a $6 million annual cost reduction effort, and authorizing up to $10 million in share buybacks. The company emphasized flexibility in capital allocation and operational adjustments across both hemispheres, aiming to maintain positive free cash flow while navigating volatility in rig counts, tariffs, and commodity prices. Management remains focused on executing its strategy and leveraging its diversified product and geographic base to sustain performance through industry headwinds.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Regeneron upgraded, AbbVie downgraded as Citi reviews policy risk
data.description:
Citi on Wednesday upgraded Regeneron (NASDAQ:REGN) and downgraded AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV), citing varying risks for the U.S. pharma majors from the Trump administration’s latest policy moves targeting the industry.

The rating changes follow President Donald Trump's executive order on Monday to reduce drug prices through a program called “Most Favored Nation,” which ties U.S. drug prices to lower prices in other developed nations.

The news of the order initially hurt the share of the global pharmaceutical companies, which had already taken a beating early this year from Trump’s oft-stated claims to subject pharmaceutical products to trade levies.

Additionally, Trump on Monday threatened to eliminate the role PBMs, also known as pharmacy benefit managers, play in the U.S. healthcare system.

Citi analyst Geoff Meacham upgraded Regeneron (NASDAQ:REGN) to Buy from Neutral, arguing that the Eylea maker’s exposure to policy risks related to tariffs and MFN discounting “seems reasonably controlled to us.”

Meacham also raised his price target on REGN to $700 from $600 per share, citing the company's strong pipeline optionality against conditions such as melanoma and an attractive risk-reward setup given its ~55% decline since the 2024 peak.

“Overall, we believe investor expectations have reset, with positive regulatory/commercial updates likely to trigger meaningful sentiment reversal,” the analyst added.

However, Meacham downgraded AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV) to Neutral from Buy, arguing that despite the company’s strong fundamentals, it faces diminishing share impact from its consistent quarterly raises going forward as investors turn attention to the Humira maker’s pipeline.

“AbbVie’s consistent 'beat and raise' quarterly trend has been a core tenet of the bull case and remains differentiated given macro uncertainties,” the analyst wrote as he slashed his price target on ABBV to $188 from $205 per share.

Comparing the company with its biopharma rivals, the analyst argued that ABBV has a lighter late-stage pipeline and faces a higher policy risk related to MFN pricing and PBM reforms, both of which threaten the company's "relative safety positioning.”
